input.background: Oral contraceptives developed in the 1960s and 1970 were associated with an increased risk of thromboembolic diseases.
input.question: Let $D$ represent thromboembolic diseases and $E$ represent exposure to early oral contraceptives. Suppose $\operatorname{Pr}(D \mid E)=0.00040$ and $\operatorname{Pr}(D \mid \bar{E})=0.00010$. Therefore, $R R=\frac{0.0004}{0.0001}=4.0$. Exposure quadrupled risk.

output.method: 事件发生概率及独立性

E.description: Indicates whether there was exposure to early oral contraceptives.

Pr(D|E).description: The probability of thromboembolic diseases given exposure to early oral contraceptives.

Pr(D|¬E).description: The probability of thromboembolic diseases given no exposure to early oral contraceptives.

RR.description: The relative risk of thromboembolic diseases due to exposure to early oral contraceptives.

meta_info.caption: Relative risk (Older formulation oral contraceptives).
meta_info.section: 5.5 More Rules and Properties of Probability
